author: hello213
title: Fk the course, fk everything in my life
comment_content: Fxxking math everywhere, I would have dropped the course if they show me those during the add drop period. Now I just want to throw myself out of the window.
comment_teaching: Most of the time I don't know what he's talking about, he assumes everyone knows math and this is probably an assumption for UST students but no I'm not maybe I should've give this an A in this category because it is my bad that I don't know math, but meh
comment_grading:
For every assignment and exam, I'm in the lower quartile. For your information, I have every math course graded below C.

I have all other non-math courses graded B or above and some other major courses graded A so I'm definitely not retarded. If you have bad math like me you should definitely drop the course now if you have enrolled.

I don't know but I'm now feeling like I am definitely not an engineer kind of person. :)
comment_workload: Not too much, but when it comes, someone like me who get an average of C and D in those year 1,2 math courses is going to suffer a lot. I would've jumped the mushroom if I have got no more things that I still want to accomplish in this world.

title: Breadth-First Exposure to Image Processing
comment_content:
The content is a breadth-first exposure to the major techniques of image processing.

Topics:
Fundamentals
- Images as functions
- Quantization
Enhancement in the Spatial Domain (Over Pixels)
- Image Histogram and Applications (Contrast Stretching, Compressing, Histogram Equalization, Histogram Specification)
- Intensity Transformation (Gray-level Transforms, Bit-Plane Slices)
- Spatial Filtering and Convolution (Gaussian and Median Filter, Unsharp Mask, Laplacian Kernel)
Enhancement in the Frequency Domain (Over Frequencies)
- (2D) (Discrete) Fourier Transform
- Smoothing and Sharpening Frequency Filters (Notch, Lowpass, Highpass, Butterworth, Gaussian, Ideal Filters, Ringing Effect)
Restoration and Filtering
- Diffusion Filtering (For edge or coherence enhancement) (Not tested)
- Random Noise (Gaussian, Rayleigh, Gamma, Uniform and Exponential Noise; Salt and Pepper Noise)
- Spatial Filters for Noise Removal (Harmonic, Contraharmonic Mean Filters; Median, Max-Min Filters; Adaptive Filters)
- Frequency Filters for Noise Removal (Bandpass, Bandreject and Notch-pass Filters)
- Restoration based on Degradation Function (Inverse, Minimum MSE Filters)
- Grey-level Assignment (Nearest-Neighbour, Bilinear Interpolation)
Morphological Image Processing
- Dilation and Erosion on Images
- Opening and Closing,
- Boundary Extraction, Connected Components and Skeleton
Color Image Processing
- RGB, CMY and HSI Color Spaces (and their inter-conversions)
- Tone and Color Correction
- Color Smoothing, Sharpening and Segmentation
- Edge Detection
- Effect of Noise on Color Channels
Image Segmentation
- Hough Circle Detection
- Edge and Line Detection
- Global Thresholding (Iterative and Statistical Approaches)
- Expectation Maximization (EM)
- Snake and Active Contour Methods
- Watershed Segmentation
Image Registration (Positional Matching)
- (2/3D) Object Translation via Matrices
- Non-Affine 2D Transforms
- Measurements of Registration Accuracy (Sum of Squared Differences, Sum of Absolute Difference, Correlation Coefficient, Mutual Information)
- Simple Optimization Methods (Powell's Method)
- Feature-based Image Stitching (SIFT with RANSAC)
Image Compression
- Redundancy and Fidelity Measures
- Encoder-Decoder Pair for Compression (Not related to Machine Learning)
- Huffman Encoding
- Lossless Predictive Coding
- Lossy Compression
- Transform Coding (Discrete Cosine Transform) and Quantization
- JPEG Compression
Image Feature Detection
- The Harris Corner Detector
- Local Binary Patterns and Rotational Invariance
- SIFT
Image Classification
- Bag of Words and Feature Extraction
- Clustering
- Principle Component Analysis for Dimensionality Reduction

Although the lectures did provide a solid mathematical background for those who prefer it, it was not strictly necessary to actually understand all the Math. Being able to understand (intuitively) what each algorithm does is far more important (at least for the course assessments). If you feel lost, try to think what each technique does on a group of pixels instead of staring at the stack of second derivatives often seen in the lecture notes - it helps with the thinking process significantly in my own experience.
comment_teaching:
Albert was a decent teacher who did manage to explain things clearly, but was sometimes unable to gauge the difficulty (and hence the time taken) when delivering the course materials.

As an example, he spent around the same time for teaching binary number conversion and some multivariable calculus-based derivation of one of the image kernels used. Coupled with my less-than-stellar Math background, this definitely confused me significantly.

Overall, the teaching was fine - perhaps more Math-focused lectures are simply not my cup of tea.
comment_grading:
Grading was... surprisingly bad for a COMP course? It was definitely a surprise (not a positive one) after grade release.

The grading scheme was as follows:
30% 3 Programming Assignments, 10% Each
[20, 25]% Midterm Exam
[45, 50]% Final Exam

The weight percentages for the exams were given as a range in the introductory PowerPoint, and they might be dynamically adjusted with respect to our performance. Although I have sent an email to the teaching team to request the exact breakdown after the semester, I did not receive a reply.

Assignments
=============
In general, the assignments were not particularly easy. They required significant time to work out the correct implementation in NumPy (although vectorization is not a requirement) or reading the OpenCV docs. Taking this course with a friend could help with checking the validity of your outputs, since (especially for Assignment 1) the expected outputs were not provided and minor implementation mistakes can lead to large output variations.

Assignment 1 was on general image processing tasks such as bit plane extraction, histogram equalization and writing your own convolution implementation using NumPy.

Assignment 2 was on color space conversion and image segmentation using the Hough circle detection algorithm and morphological image processing.

Assignment 3 was on image thresholding using EM maximization, the SIFT and RANSAC algorithms for feature detection and image stitching, and the implementation of JPEG compression. This was the most difficult of the trio since the math behind EM maximization took me significant time to comprehend.

Annoyingly, some of the assignments used minor amounts of Google Colab specific code (beyond the standard mount/change directory loop). If you wish to do your assignments locally, you might have to write some short adapters for the missing functionality.

Assignment Statistics:
Low/LQ/Mean/Median/UQ/High/Max:
1: 0/7.5/7.5/8/8/8/8
2: 0/9/8.87/10/10/10/10
3: 0/85.25/87.25/95/100/100/100

Exams
=============
Both exams tested conceptual understanding and (surprisingly) memory over raw mathematical proofs. Most of the questions mostly asked about the effects of an algorithm on an image or dry-running an algorithm on a small image, then asked you to think of the possible effects if the algorithms are slightly tweaked. There was also one question on NumPy (simple semantics only) for each exam.

In general, the final exam was more computational than the midterm (probably due to the longer time limit). The computations required were well within the range of acceptable work though (unlike COMP 4221).

Exam Statistics:
Low/LQ/Mean/Median/UQ/High/Max:
Midterm: 27/58.9/67.59/68.8/77/93/100
Final: 27.64/49.93/55.56/55.03/64.18/80.1/100

Overall Grading
=============
Scoring slightly above the estimated median yielded a B-, which is fair but less than expected - usually this translates to a B for non-Honors COMP courses in my experience.

Attached please find the approximated score distribution for your reference.
comment_workload:
Workload was moderate in general, as per a typical COMP course.

Assignments took around a week to do on-and-off, and I did not spend too much time on preparing for both exams - going through the material once and understanding what is being done should generally be sufficient.

author: Tommy Li
title: Old-school Interesting Content Taught by A Nice Professor
comment_content:
This course is mainly about standard techniques in image processing. I quite like the syllabus of the course. It covered stuff from a wide range of difficulty and some of the methods introduced is quite interesting. For example, you can segment an image with a lazy snake that tries to minimize his energy level!

List of Topics:
Topic 1: Image representation, MATLAB programming
Topic 2: Spatial domain image enhancement
Topic 3: Frequency domain image enhancement
Topic 4: Image restoration and filtering, Non-linear filtering
Topic 5: Morphological image processing
Topic 6: Image segmentation
Topic 7: Image registration
Topic 8: Image compression
Topic 9: Face detection and recognition
Topic 10: Feature descriptors

Don't get confused by the segmentation/object detection/face recognition part. This course focused on more traditional image processing based methods instead of state-of-the-art deep learning stuff. Although some of the stuff may seemed old-fashioned, they are still being widely used as of today.

Some of the research topic by the professor was also included. Nevertheless, the exam only focused on the standard stuff so you don't need to worry too much.
comment_teaching:
I don't know why but I like the professor's voice lol. Anyway, I think he did a good job in teaching. He tried to explain the slides with annotations and drawings which should not be too difficult to catch up for most students. However, he did speed up a bit in the late half of the semester so you may want to rewatch the video if you miss some of the points.

The professor did answer the question asked in class. He also don't mind to explain something again if we got confused on the concept. Overall, he is a nice prof. that explains things clearly.
comment_grading:
The grading seems quite nice from the statistics although I need more data to judge that confidently. If I assume the SD is similar to a normal COMP course exam (18~20), I would be ~1-1.2 SD above mean, which normally would be an A- or A. And, the highest score is 97 which suggests high score should be possible (not for most people tho :C). I am quite surprised to get an A+. Also, my friend got B+ with 0.5 SD above mean (with the same assumption) so it should be slightly better grading than average.

I didn't check my exam paper. But, I don't have enough time to finish the last question on AdaBoost (a standard question appeared in the assignment) and that question worth 13 points. I would say if you are not too careless, a good grade should be possible.

Grade breakdown and statistics:
Assignment 1: 100/100 (Mean: 86.6)
Assignment 2: 100/100 (Mean: 80.7)
Assignment 3: 100/100 (Mean: 82.4)
Assignment Total (40%): 40/40
Exam (60%): 84/100 (Mean: 64.6, High: 97)
Overall: 90.4% => A+

My friend: Assignment 3 deduct ~10 point + exam 73.5 => B+

Note: The weighting between 3 assignments are uneven but the exact weighting was not announced. The professor said it should be about (10%, 15%, 15%) at the start of the semester.
comment_workload:
There are total of three assignments. The assignments are not easy. You may need some time to do experiments and think deeper on how to implement the algorithm. (Simple loops will be too slow!) As the implementation is not that straight forward, there might be unexpected issues coming up. Do reserve two or three days for each of the assignments.

No past papers were given. However, do spend some time to try out (and maybe speedrun) the examples given in lectures. It is especially important in this online setting as most will got his/her pace slowed down in online exams. Try to be conservative in time management too as you cannot go back to the previous question. In fact, I got shocked when the AdaBoost (a standard but long question, pure computation) question comes up as the last question.

author: HU-HT
title: This is an ELEC course disguised as a COMP course
comment_content:
Syllabus:
- Introduction
- Fundamentals
- Image Transformation
- Spatial Filtering
- Frequency Filtering
- Image Restoration
- Color Image Processing
- Image Compression
- Morphological Image Processing
- Image Segmentation
- Image Registration
- Feature Extraction
- Image Classification
- Advanced Deep Learning in Medical Image Analysis
- Review

There are some parts that feels like teaching ahead of where it should be, such as teaching lowpass/highpass spatial filtering as filtering high/low frequency items even though the meaning is taught in the next topic.

They put COMP 2011/2012/2012H to the prerequisite, and of course the programming language they believe is best for this course is Matlab which I believe a lot of students never touched it before. From previous reviews, it seems Matlab is just for try-out implementations, so of fking course they have to put Matlab in homework and final exam wtf.

Is this a COMP, ELEC, BIEN or MATH course?

Edit: One more thing to complain. Why if we want to check the hw solution, we have to come to tutorial sessions instead of just posting for us to see? Do you use the same hw every time?
comment_teaching:
I do not like his presentation style. I attended the first few lectures, and I already feel that he doesn't know how to answer students' questions.

Since he makes everything so mathy, everything is complicated. This is especially true in morphological image processing, which I need to try out a lot of examples before I realize how simple it actually is.

I basically give up reading all the tutorial notes and only read the first one mainly teaching how to write Matlab codes.
comment_grading:
With such tight distribution, the grading will not be good regardless.
Grading scheme:
30% Homework
30% Mid-term Exam
40% Final Exam

My result:
HW 1: 95/100
(Mean: 92.48, LQ: 89.00, Median: 94.00, UQ: 98.00)
HW 2: 100/100
(Mean: 92.88, LQ: 93.00, Median: 96.00, UQ: 100.00)
HW 3: 98/100
(Mean: 92.20, LQ: 93.00, Median: 97.00, UQ: 98.00)
Mid-term Exam: 94/100
(Mean: 76.18, LQ: 65.00, Median: 79.00, UQ: 89.00)
Final Exam: 77/100
(Mean: 64.09, LQ: 56.00, Median: 66.00, UQ: 75.50)
Overall: 88.3
(Mean: 76.25, LQ: 69.30, Median: 78.70, UQ: 84.68)
Grade: A+

I'm surprised to get A+. They must have given a lot of A+ this sem, since I'm quite close to UQ in the overall score. Though since the score difference between median and UQ are quite small, I still wouldn't say it is a good grade course (especially when they put Matlab questions in final exam which definitely hurt a lot of people)
comment_workload:
The homework has an inconsistent difficulty. Sometimes you don't need to check the notes (formula literally given in the hw), but other times checking doesn't help (need seek help from DeepSeek + Wiki). What does "uncorrelated" mean in the frequency context?

Exams are hard to prepare, because although everything is mathy (and you are expected to know how to derive them), you can't really create a connection to help you memorize them. They hide the damn hw solutions. Oh don't forget to remember the method names used in the hw matlab questions because final exam explicitly tell you to use the correct specific names (and matlab questions totally worth 17 marks as well).

title: Image Processing

Poor presentation,
He expect everyone is in Math Major.

comment_grading:
Assignment is easy, so the mean is really high.

author: Icespirits
title: Interesting content, enthusiastic professor but strange exam
comment_content:
如果你是希望學習關於圖像處理以及AI圖像分析的基本原理、那這個課程應該挺適合你的。
但說真的對實在對圖像處理有興趣建議去4411, 要Grade去4511, 整體內容偏理論比較少實作，大部分工作也比較少會有相關的內容，真的不算有用。

Course Syllabus:
Ch1 Intro
Ch2 Fundamentals
Ch3 Image Transformation
Ch4 Spatial Filtering
Ch5 Frequency Filtering
Ch6 Image Restoration
Ch7 Image Color Processing
Ch8 Image Compression
Ch9 Morphological Image Processing
Ch10 Image Segmentation
Ch11 Image Registration
Ch12 Feature Extraction
Ch13 Image Classification
Ch14 Advance Deep Learning in Medical Image Analysis
comment_teaching:
新來的導師挺熱情的，但是他上課比較悶，主要就是讀slide
很好的是他有把課堂的影片放到網上，可以之後再去，然後你上課問問題他也很樂意去回答
整體來說跟大部分CS的老師差不多
comment_grading:
整體分數其實不差，問題是他的整體構成以及考試的問題出得不好。

Grading Scheme:
3 Assignment 30% Mean:92~98
Midterm 30% - Mean: 78.92 Median: 82
Final 40% - Mean: 73.51 Median: 75

剩餘grading scheme寫的功課基本大家都滿分，差別不大，而差距就是在兩個考試分出來。考試模式基本都是一個半小時五題題目，每一題大概涵蓋一至兩個主題，問題他出的試卷基本上都是四題很簡單的然後一題很難的，那四題的基本也大多只是原理不涉及數學以及理解，大部分人都是被扣了一些小分數然後就是在那一題難的上面分出來，然後一題難的通常都是要你記下來堂上的某一題公式，這就導致其實這試並沒有考你對內容的理解有多少、而是一個看你到底有沒有剛好背到那條公式的幸運轉盤、所以不是太喜歡他的Grading Scheme。

但整體來說Grade其實還算不錯、個人大部分分數都是在Mean附近，B。
comment_workload: Marking scheme寫的一樣，只有考試和三份assignment，非常輕鬆。

author: Aye
title: Image processing
comment_teaching: Good pronunciation. Topics are quite interesting and not very difficult. Some extensions were introduced although some of them are not required in exams.
comment_grading:
Average.

30% Assignments (each assignment consists of written part and programming part, of which programming part is a little harder) + 70% Exams

Exams were kind of easy.
Midterm: mean 72.1,   SD 19.3.
Final:      mean 70.07, SD 22.

100% assignments + 91% midterm + 76% final => B+
comment_workload:
Although there are lots of formulas in the notes, you do not really need to memorize them. Most of them are provided in exams. But be sure to get hold of how to use them.
If you want to learn more about computer vision, better take COMP5421. 2/3 people received A+ this semester. And the contents are really useful and challenging.

Some students took COMP4421 and COMP5421 together this semester.

author: Leoml
comment_content:
Introduction
- Fundamentals
- Image Transformation
- Spatial Filtering
- Frequency Filtering
- Image Restoration
- Color Image Processing
- Image Compression
- Morphological Image Processing
- Image Segmentation
- Image Registration
- Feature Extraction
- Image Classification
- Advanced Deep Learning in Medical Image Analysis
- Review

Some computer vision stuff and is suitable for you if you want to know the math behind image processing. There is some matlab programming needed, in homework and final exam. (Note that there is zero matlab programming in lectures and midterm but they appear in exam, you have to memorize those function names in tutorial notes and know how to apply them)
comment_teaching: Teaching does not help much. I cannot catch up even I am a Math student. You have to read the textbook or review lecture videos after lecture.
comment_grading:
Grading scheme:
30% Homework
30% Mid-term Exam
40% Final Exam
See other posts for statistics.

My score:
HW: 287/300
Midterm: 92/100
Final: 76/100
Total: 86.7
Grade: A

I am satisfied with my grade. However, I feel upset when scoring just a few marks higher already can get A+.
comment_workload: 3 homework, and they are pretty easy.

author: DrainGang
title: Decent
comment_content:
Basic introduction image processing, including spatial domain, frequency domain, image restoration and filtering, morphological image processing, image segmentation, image registration, image compression,  face recognition, etc.

All very practical and is generally interesting.
comment_teaching:
Decent.

Heavy accent however he is very enthusiastic about the lecture contents, so therefore the lectures were not too bad, however he still repeats off the powerpoint quite a lot, and since attendance is not counted a lot of people ended up skipping.

Tutorials go over using MATLAB for image processing, however the homework and exams do not cover the contents this semester, so only attend if you are interested.
comment_grading:
Acceptable for COMP standards.

30% Midterm: Questions that are basically the same as ones seen in assignments, however it is easy to make careless mistakes, so be careful! Averages are very high (79/100)

40% Final: Exactly the same as the midterm, all based on assignments and therefore averages are again very high (73.5/100)

30% Assignments: 3 assignments, each worth 10%, most questions can be answered by looking into the lecture notes, and for those that cannot, answers can be found on the internet, averages are almost full mark on all 3.

The complex math shown in the lectures ended up being very basic and easy calculations, so this course is more about being careful and studying the assignments.

0.5% less than mean overall, B-, as expected.
comment_workload:
Quite low for a 4000 level course.

Assignments are very easy and takes less than a day to complete, and for the exams since none of the tutorial content is covered and all questions are based on assignments and lecture notes, studying for the exam is also not too difficult.

author: HinJaiiii
title: A relatively light workload 4000-level course
comment_content:
The content is interesting. It taught some basic techniques in  image processing. The final project was either a survey project or a research project, which I chose to evaluate existing methods or to propose new methods on a problem in the research one.

Introduction to image processing. Topics include image processing and analysis in spatial and frequency domains, image restoration and compression, image segmentation and registration, morphological image processing, representation and description, object recognition, related application areas and some other closely related topics. Some sophisticated image processing and analysis tools and state-of-the-art methods may also be introduced subject to the availability of time.
comment_teaching: It was the first time the professor taught this course. The professor was passionate in delivering the course. He tried to encourage interaction in class. Also, he is a kind person and considerate to the students; at one time, he postponed the deadline the final project to provide the students more breathing time. However, he does not speak good English, which may affect how the students understand the materials.
comment_grading: The assignments and exams were a bit too easy and could not differentiate the students' grades too much. The grading of the project might have a greater impact. I did not do pretty well in the group project, even I have good scores in the exams, I received B range at the end.
comment_workload: There were a total of three assignments. The questions were straightforward, and the length is acceptable, each just took me as much as a day to finish. The mid-term and final were super EAZY. Some of the questions are just variants of the ones in the assignments. However, the group project needed some considerable amount of effort and time.

author: ckhn
title: Interesting course with good teaching and support
comment_content: This course covers some basic and interesting topic on image processing, including spatial domain, frequency domain, image restoration and filtering, morphological image processing, image segmentation, image registration, image compression,  face recognition, etc.
comment_teaching: Prof. Chung is an expert in this area. He can explain the concepts clearly at good pace. He is also nice and chats with students after the course. TAs are also very helpful and respond quickly to my questions on assignment, even I asked during weekend.
comment_grading: I got an excellent grading from this course I have pretty good result in the final exam but I can't finish the second assignment. So this course does not require students to be perfect.
comment_workload: There are 3 assignments and final exam. The first 2 assignments consist of many questions and some high level programming to test your concepts of image processing. The difficulty of the last assignment is tuned to be much easier as many students could barely finish the second one. I appreciate that the difficulty of assignment is adjusted according to the ability and tight of schedule.

author: Mr BIGPIG
title: Good Grade
comment_content: 關於圖像處理同埋一d 識別系統原理
comment_teaching: 教學方面唔錯~ 經常會畫圖講解 正常有留心聽都會明距講咩

功課方面就比較高分 avg大概B

得Final exam 無mid term

author: ymji
title: Image processing
comment_content: Content is about filtering like Gaussian, median, mean and also about edge detection, boundaries, segmentation etc.
comment_teaching: professor is smart but can get easily boring. His voice makes me hard to pay attention to the class and because I am also taking another class that is similar to image processing, I know the material already and that makes me hard to focus in this course.
comment_grading:
Not too bad. If you do not make any mistake on homework, easily full mark. A small mistake can take off big points because each question has grading criteria. Even though your code runs appropriately, if you made a small mistake and had a wrong result, that will take off the whole point in that criteria, so make sure you double check your code and output.
three homework and final
comment_workload: not much of workload. He provides some code and we have to fill out rest of the code to run the image processing. Can be easily done. Homework isn't too hard. Didn't take the exam yet, so I do not know much about how exam will be like.

author: gossipgirl
comment_content: Content is okayish. Not that great, not very bad either. Some interesting stuff like face recognition, segmentation, edge detection, boundaries, skeletons, etc.
comment_teaching:
Not that good. Lectures are pretty boring. However, what I actually didnt like about the course/teaching was that none of the assignments were related to any exam questions. All assignments were 90% coding.
It didn't really prepare me well for the exams.
No solutions of assignments/exams released. No past papers, very few sample questions discussed during the sem. Not a great experience altogether.
comment_grading:
Grading isn't too bad.
Course mean 70% --> B

Assignments 30%     mean 26/30
Midterm 23%            mean 15/23
Final 47%                  mean 29/47
comment_workload: Not too much workload. 3 assignments + 2 exams

author: The_Unknown
title: Little room for error due to high median
comment_content:
A standard introduction to image processing techniques.

Frankly speaking, the course is like an overview on the techniques related to image processing. So, it is easier than a typical level 4 elective. That is also why it has an extremely high mean/median score.

However, if we are to consider the number of chapters in the course and the semester duration, I would say that the content is rather appropriate.

Chapters include the following:

Introduction
Image Fundamentals
Image Transformations
Image Filtering
Image Restoration
Image Compression
Morphological Image Processing
Image Segmentation
Image Registration
Feature Extraction
Image Recognition and Deep Learning
Advanced Topics in Medical Image Processing
comment_teaching:
Professor Chen speaks rather fast, so it is a bit hard to follow when in the lecture room, since notes are released just a day early.
But luckily, lectures videos are present in canvas. Once you read  the notes and watch the videos, it is easy to understand.
comment_grading:
The mean/median of this course is high, like really high for assignments and midterms. So you need to be careful about your answers.

This is especially true for assignments where some questions are kinda out of lecture scope. To be fair, they do provide hints for questions like that.

Finals, on the other hand, provide more flexibility since mean/median is more "typical".

To break it down:
Assignment 1 (10%): Median 94, High 100
Assignment 2 (10%): Median 100, High 100
Assignment 3 (10%): Median 100, High 100
Midterm (30%): Median 82, High 100
Final Exam (40%): Median 75, High 97

My weighted overall is around 95, which translates to an A+.

Some questions in exam relate to details mentioned in small font/images (like the phase angle) and are easy to be missed. So, do remember to clearly look at the images and their labels because they may be asked in exam.
comment_workload:
The only workload comes from the assignment, which should take about two hours or three if you are unfamiliar with the lecture notes and less if you are familiar.
Given that each assignment has just 4-5 questions, I would say the workload is appropriate.

That said, you will likely need to study the lecture notes in depth.
